IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles the important and widely-recognized problem of mention ambiguity in joint text–knowledge representation ("the same mention phrase usually refer to various entities") and proposes MPME to learn multiple sense embeddings per mention by jointly modeling textual contexts and KB entities. Its phrase-level, multi-prototype approach (vs. single-prototype alignment) and an efficient language-model-based disambiguation that achieves state-of-the-art on an entity linking benchmark suggest the results will influence how researchers represent and disambiguate mentions. This is likely to be adopted or extended in related areas (WSD, entity linking, KG completion), opening new practical directions while having broad applicability across NLP tasks.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The manuscript states its main claims (a Multi-Prototype Mention Embedding model and an efficient language-model based disambiguator that achieves state-of-the-art on entity linking) but provides no supporting technical detail in the provided text: there are no model architectures, objective functions, mathematical derivations, or quantitative experimental results or statistics shown. The paper also lacks experimental methodology (datasets, baselines, training/evaluation protocol) and thus the SOTA and quality claims are unsupported. Given these significant missing technical evidences and assumptions, the work cannot be judged correct as presented.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The manuscript claims "qualitative and quantitative analysis" and reports achieving "state-of-the-art" on an entity linking benchmark, but the provided text contains no experimental details, datasets, metrics, baselines or results tables to support these claims, so experiments are shallow. No ablation or sensitivity analyses are described. Related work appears reasonably covered (many citations to Wang et al., Yamada et al., WSD and entity linking literature), but failure cases or limitations are not discussed and there is insufficient methodological detail (model architectures, training/hyperparameters) to reproduce the work.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper proposes a Multi-Prototype Mention Embedding (MPME) model that jointly models words from textual contexts and entities from a knowledge base to learn multiple sense embeddings for mentions, which is squarely within core NLP/representation learning (text & KB integration) research. It targets entity linking and WSD, reports qualitative and quantitative analyses and a language-model-based disambiguation method with claimed state-of-the-art on benchmark datasets—matching the evaluation standards expected at top-tier NLP/ML venues. The work explicitly builds on and compares to relevant prior art (e.g., Wang et al., Yamada et al.) and addresses an audience concerned with embeddings, disambiguation, and KB-text integration, so its scope and positioning are appropriate for such conferences.

CLARITY.justification: The document is poorly presented: many sentences and citations are duplicated verbatim (e.g., repeated lines about expensive training and WSD references), and there are clear typos/encoding issues such as “compon n s”, “i text model”, and “singleprototype”. The main contribution is stated in the abstract (the Multi-Prototype Mention Embedding model and a language-model disambiguation approach), but the earlier text is noisy and disrupts logical flow so organization is hard to follow. No figures/tables are shown, and no formal notation is introduced or consistently defined, so readers would struggle to parse methods and experiments.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper cites and builds on many relevant prior works (e.g. Wang et al., 2014; Yamada et al., 2016; Han et al., 2016) and explains a clear conceptual difference (MPME vs single-prototype alignment). It also claims quantitative evaluation and state-of-the-art on a benchmark. However, the provided text contains no concrete experimental comparisons, baseline lists, metrics, or result tables, nor acknowledgment of when prior methods match or beat the proposal, so fairness and detailed analysis of differences cannot be verified.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes Multi-Prototype Mention Embeddings (MPME) that learns multiple sense embeddings per mention by jointly modeling textual contexts and KB entities, and adds a language-model-based disambiguation step (text: "novel Multi-Prototype Mention Embedding model... learns multiple sense embeddings"; "efficient language model based approach to disambiguate"). This is a clear extension of prior joint text–knowledge embedding and multi-prototype word work (cites Wang et al. 2014; Yamada et al. 2016; Neelakantan et al. 2014) and explicitly departs from single-prototype alignment (text: "rather than aligning between singleprototype word embeddings and entity embeddings"). The idea is a non-trivial, useful adaptation but appears incremental relative to well-established multi-prototype and joint-embedding lines, so novelty is moderate rather than highly original.
